title: Invasive non-typhoidal salmonella deaths in children under five
subtitle: Estimated annual number of deaths from [invasive non-typhoidal salmonella](#dod:non-typhoidal-salmonella) in children under five.
caption:
Invasive non-typhoidal salmonella deaths in children under five

Estimated annual number of deaths from invasive non-typhoidal salmonella in children under five.

This choropleth world map displays the estimated annual number of deaths from invasive non-typhoidal salmonella in children under five for the year 2023. The legend uses a color scale ranging from light yellow for zero deaths to dark brown for 30,000 deaths, with intermediate thresholds at 10, 30, 100, 300, 1,000, 3,000, and 10,000. The highest mortality rates are concentrated in West and Central Africa. Mali, Burkina Faso, and Niger appear in the darkest brown shades, indicating values in the highest bands between 10,000 and 30,000 deaths. Surrounding countries in the region, such as Nigeria, Ghana, and Cameroon, display dark orange hues that correspond to the 1,000 to 3,000 and 3,000 to 10,000 ranges. In Southern and Eastern Africa, nations like the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Tanzania, and Mozambique show mid-range orange colors, falling generally between 300 and 3,000 deaths. In Asia, India is visible in a lighter orange shade, suggesting a value between 100 and 300, while Pakistan and Bangladesh appear in slightly darker bands. Most of South America, North America, Europe, and Australia are colored in the lightest yellow shades, representing values near zero or below 10 deaths. Greenland and a few other territories are marked with a hatched pattern, denoting no data. The visual distribution highlights a heavy burden of these specific deaths in the African continent compared to the rest of the world.
